mnemonics.title: DB Characteristics Mnemonic
mnemonics.content: SHARED: Security, High-level query language, Abstraction, Redundancy control, Efficient access, Data independence - the 6 key characteristics of a DBMS

mnemonics.title: ACID Properties
mnemonics.content: A.C.I.D. = Atomic (all-or-nothing), Consistent (valid state), Isolated (concurrent independence), Durable (permanent results)

mnemonics.title: DB Purpose Memory Hook
mnemonics.content: Purpose = S.C.R.U.D. - Store, Control, Retrieve, Update, Data integrity - why we use databases

mnemonics.title: Data Independence Levels
mnemonics.content: Physical independence happens first, then logical. Think: P.L. = Physical comes before Logical in the acronym

acronyms.usage: Software that enables creation, maintenance, and manipulation of databases - the interface between users and the database
A DBMS based on the relational model, storing data in tables with rows and columns (e.g., MySQL, Oracle)
Four properties ensuring reliable transaction processing in databases
Commands like CREATE, ALTER, DROP that define the database structure/schema
Commands like SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE for manipulating data in the database
Person responsible for managing, securing, and maintaining the database system

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a well-organized filing cabinet with labeled folders (tables), each containing related documents (rows) with specific fields (columns). The cabinet drawer index (database schema) tells you where everything goes.

Think of database tables as Excel spreadsheets - rows represent individual records, columns represent attributes, and the header row defines the schema. Multiple related spreadsheets (tables) form a database.

Draw three stacked circles: External (user views) on top, Conceptual (logical structure) in middle, Physical (storage) on bottom. Arrows show independence flowing upward - changes below don't affect above.
Picture a shield with four quadrants: A for Atomic (unbreakable), C for Consistent (checkmark), I for Isolated (walls between), D for Durable (rock). This shield protects your data transactions.

analogies.analogy: Using a file system for data is like having a messy pile of papers - you manually search, risk duplicates, and face security issues. A database is like a well-organized library with a librarian (DBMS) who finds exactly what you need, keeps everything secure, and handles multiple people at once.

Physical data independence is like renovating your house's foundation without affecting the room layout (logical). Logical independence is like rearranging rooms without breaking the foundation. This separation protects your investment (data).

Multiple users accessing a database simultaneously is like cars at an intersection. The DBMS uses traffic signals (locks/timestamps) to prevent collisions, ensuring orderly access and preventing data crashes.

analogies.concept: Database Normalization as Organizing a Closet
analogies.analogy: Just as you organize clothes by type, color, and season in a closet, normalization organizes data into related tables to eliminate redundancy - each item (data) has its proper place, making retrieval efficient and updates easier.
